summary: Gruber challenges a New York Times claim that Apple product announcements under Steve Jobs 'routinely sent its stock soaring,' while under Tim Cook the stock fell. He methodically documents Apple's stock price after every major Jobs-era product announcement from 2001 to 2010, showing that the stock actually fell after most of them — including the iPod, iPod Mini, MacBook Air, and iPhone 3G introductions. The piece demonstrates that Wall Street tends to be conservative and has never reliably rewarded Apple's product innovation with immediate stock gains. Gruber argues this narrative of Cook-era decline versus Jobs-era triumph is factually wrong and provably so.
key_points: Apple's stock fell after the majority of Steve Jobs's major product announcements, including the original iPod, iPod Mini, and MacBook Air introductions
Even the original iPad announcement was followed by days of stock decline, contradicting the narrative that Jobs-era launches sent shares soaring
Wall Street tends to reward the conventional and punish the unconventional, making stock price a poor proxy for innovation quality
The 'Apple is lost without Jobs' narrative was being inserted as cookie-cutter filler into otherwise serious journalism
Vogelstein's own acknowledgment that Jobs produced revolutionary products every 3-5 years undermines his criticism of Cook after only two years
money_quotes: For all the lip service paid to 'innovation', Wall Street tends to be conservative, rewarding the conventional and punishing the unconventional.
It's wrong, factually and provably wrong, to claim that 'Apple product announcements used to routinely send its stock soaring.'
If anything, the opposite has been true: Apple stock has routinely fallen after product announcements, including the entire final decade of Steve Jobs's career.

The 10 percent drop in Apple's share price on the day of the 5S/5C introduction is certainly unusual, but everything about Apple's share price the last few years has been unusual, particularly its volatility.
Wall Street does not understand the iPhone.
You can actually check these things. Stock prices and the dates of Steve Jobs's product announcements are matters of fact.
key_insight: The claim that Apple's stock soared after Jobs-era product launches is factually false — the stock routinely fell, making stock price a useless metric for comparing Jobs and Cook's leadership.
